title: Whether grief has an expiration date in the workplace
summary: Argue whether organisations should set limits on how long employees' grief is accommodated, or whether such limits are harmful.
topic: Whether grief has an expiration date in the workplace
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring whether workplaces should — or inevitably do — impose an informal expiration date on an employee's grief, and whether doing so causes more harm than the alternatives. Consider the difference between accommodating loss and requiring performance of recovery. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete case (a specific policy, a cross-cultural comparison, a documented workplace experience). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What organisations are actually managing when they impose bereavement timelines is not grief but…
Granted, no employer can restructure its entire operation around the unpredictable duration of…
Having returned to the office within the allotted two weeks, many bereaved employees find themselves…

khung thời gian của nỗi đau / lịch trình đau buồn
chấp nhận và tạo điều kiện cho sự mất mát

nỗi đau không được thừa nhận — mất mát không được xã hội công nhận là đau thương

bị kỳ thị / bị gắn mác xấu

đồng lõa trong

năm giai đoạn của nỗi đau (mô hình Kübler-Ross)
gắn cờ / đánh dấu hiệu suất của nhân viên là đáng lo ngại
bệnh lý hóa — coi điều gì đó bình thường là bệnh lý
chương trình chăm sóc sức khỏe tại nơi làm việc

hintVocab.phrase: to be left to navigate alone
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị bỏ lại để tự tìm đường một mình
văn hóa quản lý
nghỉ phép nhân đạo

sampleText:
What organisations are actually managing when they impose bereavement timelines is not grief but the appearance of grief — the point at which a loss becomes visible enough to disrupt output. Most corporate bereavement policies allow three to five days for the death of an immediate family member. What the research on mourning has consistently shown is that acute grief typically peaks not in the first week but in the weeks and months that follow, once the practical demands of a death have been resolved and the absence becomes fully real.

The three-day limit is not designed around how humans grieve. It is designed around a calendar. Having returned to the office within the allotted period, many bereaved employees find themselves managing a second burden: the expectation that they will perform normal function in full view of colleagues who have moved on. The grief has not ended; it has simply become invisible, and invisibility is not recovery. It is suppression rebranded as professionalism.

Granted, no employer can restructure its entire operation around the unpredictable duration of any individual's loss. Teams depend on coverage, deadlines do not pause for personal circumstances, and most organisations operate with staffing margins that make extended absences genuinely disruptive. These are real constraints, and any account of workplace grief that ignores them fails to engage with how organisations actually function.

Even so, the solution to those constraints is flexibility, not false timelines. Several Scandinavian employers have introduced rolling grief support arrangements, under which an employee whose performance is affected by loss is offered adjusted duties and regular check-ins for up to six months rather than being flagged as underperforming. The cost of those arrangements has been consistently reported as lower than the cost of turnover caused by employees who felt inadequately supported and resigned. Supporting grief, it turns out, is not a welfare luxury — it is workforce retention.

The deeper problem with expiration dates on grief is that they do not stop the grief; they stop the organisation from seeing it. Employees who are still mourning six weeks after a loss do not stop mourning because the HR calendar has moved on. They stop showing it. That suppression carries its own costs: reduced concentration, withdrawal, a kind of presenteeism in which a person is physically present and emotionally elsewhere. A workplace that cannot accommodate visible grief does not have less grief — it has more grief performed as competence, and that performance serves no one.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the point at which a loss becomes visible enough to disrupt output

sampleAnnotations.phrase: acute grief typically peaks not in the first week but in the weeks and months that follow

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose what is really being managed
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What organisations are actually managing when they impose bereavement timelines is not grief but the appearance of grief' dùng 'What X manages is not Y but Z' để bác bỏ cái tên chính thức (quản lý nỗi đau) và thay thế bằng thực tế (quản lý vẻ ngoài của nỗi đau).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What organisations are actually managing … is not grief but the appearance of grief' uses a what-cleft to expose the gap between what policies claim to address (grief) and what they actually manage (its visibility) — placing the crucial distinction at the sentence's end.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause to establish prior condition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having returned to the office within the allotted period, many bereaved employees find themselves managing a second burden' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành để nén sự kiện trước (trở lại văn phòng) vào một cụm từ, rồi mệnh đề chính nêu hậu quả.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having returned to the office within the allotted period, many bereaved employees find themselves …' compresses the prior event (returning) into a participial clause, making the main clause — the second burden — feel like its direct consequence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều các tổ chức thực sự quản lý khi áp đặt khung thời gian tang lễ không phải là nỗi đau mà là vẻ ngoài của nỗi đau — thời điểm mà sự mất mát trở nên đủ rõ ràng để làm gián đoạn sản lượng. Hầu hết các chính sách tang lễ doanh nghiệp cho phép ba đến năm ngày cho cái chết của một thành viên gia đình trực tiếp. Điều mà nghiên cứu về tang lễ đã liên tục chỉ ra là nỗi đau cấp tính thường đạt đỉnh điểm không phải trong tuần đầu tiên mà trong các tuần và tháng tiếp theo, một khi những yêu cầu thực tế của một cái chết đã được giải quyết và sự vắng mặt trở nên hoàn toàn thực.

Giới hạn ba ngày không được thiết kế xung quanh cách con người đau buồn. Nó được thiết kế xung quanh một lịch. Sau khi trở lại văn phòng trong thời gian được phân bổ, nhiều nhân viên đang để tang thấy mình phải quản lý một gánh nặng thứ hai: kỳ vọng rằng họ sẽ thực hiện chức năng bình thường trước mắt đồng nghiệp đã tiếp tục tiến lên. Nỗi đau chưa kết thúc; nó chỉ đơn giản là trở nên vô hình, và sự vô hình không phải là phục hồi. Đó là sự kìm nén được đổi tên thành tính chuyên nghiệp.

Thừa nhận, không có chủ lao động nào có thể tái cơ cấu toàn bộ hoạt động xung quanh thời gian khó đoán của sự mất mát của bất kỳ cá nhân nào. Các nhóm phụ thuộc vào sự bao phủ, thời hạn không dừng lại vì hoàn cảnh cá nhân, và hầu hết các tổ chức hoạt động với biên độ nhân sự khiến cho việc nghỉ dài hạn thực sự gây rối loạn. Đây là những ràng buộc thực tế, và bất kỳ câu chuyện nào về nỗi đau tại nơi làm việc bỏ qua chúng đều không tham gia với cách các tổ chức thực sự hoạt động.

Dẫu vậy, giải pháp cho những ràng buộc đó là sự linh hoạt, không phải khung thời gian giả tạo. Một số nhà tuyển dụng Scandinavia đã giới thiệu các thỏa thuận hỗ trợ đau buồn liên tục, theo đó một nhân viên có hiệu suất bị ảnh hưởng bởi mất mát được cung cấp nhiệm vụ điều chỉnh và kiểm tra thường xuyên trong tối đa sáu tháng thay vì bị gắn cờ là làm việc dưới mức. Chi phí của những thỏa thuận đó đã được báo cáo nhất quán là thấp hơn chi phí nghỉ việc do nhân viên cảm thấy không được hỗ trợ đầy đủ và từ chức. Hỗ trợ nỗi đau, hóa ra, không phải là xa xỉ phẩm phúc lợi — đó là giữ chân lực lượng lao động.

Vấn đề sâu sắc hơn với thời hạn sử dụng của nỗi đau là chúng không ngăn nỗi đau; chúng ngăn tổ chức nhìn thấy nó. Nhân viên vẫn còn đang để tang sáu tuần sau khi mất không ngừng đau buồn vì lịch nhân sự đã tiến lên. Họ ngừng thể hiện nó. Sự kìm nén đó mang theo những chi phí riêng của nó: giảm sự tập trung, thu mình lại, một loại hiện diện giả trong đó một người hiện diện về mặt thể chất nhưng vắng mặt về mặt cảm xúc. Một nơi làm việc không thể chấp nhận nỗi đau nhìn thấy được không có ít đau buồn hơn — nó có nhiều hơn nỗi đau được biểu diễn như năng lực, và sự biểu diễn đó không phục vụ ai.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose the gap between a policy's stated purpose and its real function?
options: What organisations are actually managing when they impose bereavement timelines is not grief but the appearance of grief.
Organisations manage grief through bereavement timelines which appear to function as policies.
It is not grief but appearance that bereavement timelines are managing in organisations.
Bereavement timelines manage what appearances of grief organisations impose.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft delays the crucial reframing — 'not grief but the appearance of grief' — to the end, exposing the contradiction in the policy.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is not … but …' with the key distinction at the very end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a perfect participle clause to establish a prior condition and its consequence?
options: Having returned to the office within the allotted period, many bereaved employees find themselves managing a second burden.
Many bereaved employees having returned allotted period find themselves second burden managing.
The allotted period having returned employees bereaved find managing second burden office.
Returned having to office allotted period employees bereaved find themselves burden managing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having returned …' establishes the prior event; the main clause describes the consequence — both subjects are the same ('bereaved employees').
mcQuestions.hint: The participle clause subject must match the main clause subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'presenteeism' mean in a workplace context?
options: The practice of arriving early and staying late to impress managers
Being physically present at work while unable to function effectively, often due to illness or emotional distress
A formal policy requiring all employees to attend weekly team meetings
The obligation to be visible on video calls during remote working hours
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Presenteeism' describes the phenomenon of being at work in body but not in mind — the essay links it to employees who are still grieving but no longer able to show it.
mcQuestions.hint: The opposite of 'absenteeism' — but does physical presence equal actual productivity?

options: Grief that is too intense to be managed through normal coping strategies
Loss that is not publicly recognised as legitimate, leaving the person without social support
A formal psychiatric diagnosis for people who cannot grieve normally
Grief experienced by employees who lose their jobs rather than a person
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Disenfranchised grief' (Kenneth Doka's term) describes losses that society does not validate as 'real' grief — such as the death of a colleague, a pet, or an estranged parent.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Disenfranchised' means denied a legitimate claim — whose grief is left without acknowledgement?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what has been consistently found in research?
options: Researchers consistently show that acute grief peaks weeks after the death.
Acute grief has been consistently shown to peak in the weeks and months after the first week.
The consistent showing of acute grief peaking weeks months research.
Research consistently peaks showing acute grief months weeks first.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has been consistently shown' uses the passive to foreground what research reveals, keeping 'acute grief' in subject position rather than 'researchers'.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts 'acute grief' as the subject without naming the researchers?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'suppressiont rebranded as professionalism' mean?
options: A company policy that requires employees to be trained in emotional management
The practice of hiding or denying grief at work while it is framed by the organisation as professional conduct
A formal suppression of workplace rights disguised as a development opportunity
The renaming of a wellbeing programme to make it sound more corporate
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that requiring employees to hide grief and function normally is really emotional suppression, but it is framed by workplace culture as professional behaviour.
mcQuestions.hint: What happens when something harmful (suppression) is given a positive name (professionalism)?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what did some Scandinavian employers introduce to address workplace grief?
options: A strict three-day bereavement leave policy enforced across all departments
Rolling grief support arrangements offering adjusted duties and check-ins for up to six months
A mandatory referral to an external therapist after any bereavement
An anonymous reporting system for employees concerned about grieving colleagues
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'Several Scandinavian employers have introduced rolling grief support arrangements, under which an employee … is offered adjusted duties and regular check-ins for up to six months.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the Scandinavian example in the fourth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'flagged' in its workplace meaning?
options: Being flagged as underperforming is the alternative to receiving rolling grief support.
Employees flagged underperforming as being grief rolling support receiving.
Rolling support flagged employees grief being underperforming receiving.
Underperforming employees being flagged as receiving grief rolling support.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being flagged as X' (passive gerund) means being identified and marked as X — here, being identified as underperforming rather than supported.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'flagged as' in a passive structure with the correct subject?

options: Grief that progresses steadily through predictable stages in order
Grief that does not follow a fixed sequence and may intensify unexpectedly, revisit stages, or peak at unpredicted moments
The experience of multiple simultaneous losses that cannot be separated
A formal therapeutic model for processing complicated bereavement
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Non-linear' means not following a straight, predictable line — grief research since the 1990s has moved away from stage models, recognising that grief is unpredictable and recursive.
mcQuestions.hint: If 'linear' means progressing in a straight, predictable sequence, what does 'non-linear' mean?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main argument about grief support in the workplace?
options: Grief is a private matter and should not be accommodated at work at all.
Flexible grief support is both more humane and more cost-effective than rigid timelines.
All employees should receive unlimited paid leave following any bereavement.
Grief is too individual to be addressed by any workplace policy.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that flexible support (e.g. Scandinavian models) costs less than turnover and is more effective than false timelines — making it both the ethical and practical choice.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence: 'Supporting grief, it turns out, is not a welfare luxury — it is workforce retention.'

options: The physical exhaustion caused by overwork in a demanding job
The managed expression or suppression of feelings as part of doing one's job
A training programme designed to improve communication between managers and teams
The stress caused by receiving negative feedback from a manager
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Emotional labour' (Arlie Hochschild's concept) describes the work of managing one's feelings and expressions to fulfil the emotional requirements of a role.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a customer service worker who must smile even when upset — what kind of effort is that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best introduces a concession before returning to the main argument?
options: Granted, no employer can restructure its entire operation around the unpredictable duration of any individual's loss.
Therefore, no employer can restructure its entire operation around unpredictable losses.
As a result, no employer can restructure its operation around any individual's loss.
In conclusion, no employer can restructure its operation around unpredictable loss duration.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' signals that the essay is acknowledging a valid opposing point before pivoting with 'Even so, …'
mcQuestions.hint: Which opener most clearly signals 'I accept this point against my argument but…'?

options: The practice of retraining existing employees after a role changes
The ability of an organisation to keep its employees rather than losing them to other employers
A legal requirement to re-hire employees who were made redundant
The process of tracking employee attendance and punctuality
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Retention' means keeping; 'workforce retention' is the set of practices and conditions that reduce employee turnover.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Retain' means to keep — what is being kept in 'workforce retention'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains formal academic register throughout?
options: Honestly, making people hide their grief at work is just cruel and everyone knows it.
Companies are basically telling grieving employees to get over it in three days which is insane.
A workplace that cannot accommodate visible grief does not have less grief — it has more grief performed as competence.
Like, grief support programmes are actually cheaper than people leaving so why don't more companies do it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise, formal language ('accommodate', 'visible grief', 'performed as competence') in a balanced, analytical structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, when does research suggest acute grief typically peaks?
options: In the first 24 to 48 hours after a death
During the funeral and immediate arrangements
In the weeks and months after the first week, once practical demands have been resolved
After six months, when the employee has had time to fully process the loss
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'acute grief typically peaks not in the first week but in the weeks and months that follow, once the practical demands of a death have been resolved and the absence becomes fully real.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the first paragraph — when does the research say grief peaks?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'grief performed as competence'?
options: A training programme in which employees learn to manage grief effectively
The act of suppressing visible grief and appearing professionally functional when one is still mourning
A formal assessment process for measuring an employee's resilience
The professional skill of remaining calm during difficult conversations at work
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that when organisations impose grief timelines, employees do not stop grieving — they learn to 'perform' normalcy, hiding their real state behind the appearance of competence.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final sentence: 'more grief performed as competence, and that performance serves no one.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever had to hide a difficult emotion — grief, fear, anger — because you were in a professional setting? What did that concealment cost you, and what would it have meant to have it acknowledged?
